en.extract:
The yellow-bellied flyrobin (Cryptomicroeca flaviventris) is a species of passerine bird in the Australasian robin family Petroicidae. It is the only species in the genus Cryptomicroeca. The yellow-bellied flyrobin is endemic to New Caledonia, where it occurs on the island of Grande Terre. It occupies a range of habitats, including dry lowlands, woodland, Pinus and Pandanus forest, and humid forest from sea level up to 1,525 m (5,000 ft).


Taxonomy
The yellow-bellied flyrobin was described in 1860 by the French ornithologists, Jules Verreaux and Oeillet des Murs, from a specimen collected in New Caledonia. They coined the binomial name Eopsaltria flavigastra. The English ornithologist, Richard Bowdler Sharpe, realised that the specific epithet was preoccupied, and in 1903 he proposed flaviventris as a replacement. The species was long considered one of the yellow robins of the genus Eopsaltria. However, a 2009 genetic study showed it to be nested within the flyrobin genus Microeca, and hence it was moved to Microeca, and its common name was changed from yellow-bellied robin to yellow-bellied flyrobin in the online list of world birds maintained by Frank Gill and David Donsker on behalf of the International Ornithological Committee (IOC). A more comprehensive genetic study of the family Petroicidae, published in 2011, found that the yellow-bellied flyrobin was divergent from the other members of Microeca, and instead was sister to a clade containing the Microeca and the torrent flyrobin. The yellow-bellied flyrobin is now placed as the only species in the genus Cryptomicroeca that was introduced in 2012.


Description
The yellow-bellied robin is a medium-sized Australasian robin, 14–15 cm (5.5–5.9 in) in length and weighing around 12 g (0.42 oz). The plumage is similar to members of the genus Eopsaltria: dark olive-grey back, tail and wings, grey head and chest with a slightly lighter throat, and yellow belly and rump. The legs are grey.


References

BirdLife International 2004.  Eopsaltria flaviventris.   2006 IUCN Red List of Threatened Species.   Downloaded on 25 July 2007.

The '''yellow-bellied flyrobin''' ('''''Cryptomicroeca flaviventris''''') is a species of [[passerine]] bird in the Australasian robin family [[Petroicidae]]. It is the only species in the genus '''''Cryptomicroeca'''''. The yellow-bellied flyrobin is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[New Caledonia]], where it occurs on the island of [[Grande Terre (New Caledonia)|Grande Terre]]. It occupies a range of habitats, including dry lowlands, woodland, ''[[Pinus]]'' and ''[[Pandanus]]'' forest, and humid forest from sea level up to {{cvt|1525|m|-2|abbr=on}}.

==Taxonomy==
The yellow-bellied flyrobin was described in 1860 by the French ornithologists, [[Jules Verreaux]] and [[Marc Athanase Parfait Œillet des Murs|Oeillet des Murs]], from a specimen collected in [[New Caledonia]]. They coined the [[binomial name]] ''Eopsaltria flavigastra''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Verreaux | first1=Jules | author1-link=Jules Verreaux | last2=des Murs | first2=O.| author2-link=Marc Athanase Parfait Œillet des Murs | year=1860 | title=Description d'oiseaux nouveaux de la Nouvelle-Calédonie et indication des espèces déjà connues de ce pays | journal=Revue et magasin de zoologie pure et appliquée | language=fr, la | series=2nd series | volume=12 | pages=383–396 [392–393] | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2264788 }}</ref> The English ornithologist, [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]], realised that the [[specific epithet]] was [[preoccupied]], and in 1903 he proposed ''flaviventris'' as a replacement.<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Sharpe | first1=R. Bowdler | author-link1=Richard Bowdler Sharpe | year=1903 | title=A Hand-list of the Genera and Species of Birds | volume=4 | location=London | publisher=British Museum | page=315 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8391646 }}</ref> The species was long considered one of the yellow robins of the genus ''[[Eopsaltria]]''.<ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1986 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=11 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=573 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14484274 }}</ref> However, a 2009 genetic study showed it to be nested within the flyrobin genus ''[[Microeca]]'',<ref>{{cite journal|title=Multi-locus phylogeny clarifies the systematics of the Australo-Papuan robins (Family Petroicidae, Passeriformes) |last1=Loynes | first1=K. |last2=Joseph | first2=L. |last3=Keogh | first3=J.S. |year=2009 | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=53 | issue=1 | pages=212–219 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2009.05.012 | pmid=19463962 |bibcode=2009MolPE..53..212L }}</ref> and hence it was moved to ''Microeca'', and its common name was changed from yellow-bellied robin to yellow-bellied flyrobin in the online list of world birds maintained by [[Frank Gill (ornithologist)|Frank Gill]] and David Donsker on behalf of the [[International Ornithological Committee]] (IOC).<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2019 | title=Australasian robins, rockfowl, rockjumpers, Rail-babbler | work=World Bird List Version 9.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/au_robins/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=18 June 2019 }}</ref> A more comprehensive genetic study of the family [[Petroicidae]], published in 2011, found that the yellow-bellied flyrobin was divergent from the other members of ''Microeca'', and instead was [[sister taxon|sister]] to a clade containing the ''[[Microeca]]'' and the [[torrent flyrobin]].<ref name=christidis>{{ cite journal | last1=Christidis | first1=L. | last2=Irestedt | first2=M. | last3=Rowe | first3=D. | last4=Boles | first4=W.E. | last5=Norman | first5=J.A. | year=2011 | title=Mitochondrial and nuclear DNA phylogenies reveal a complex evolutionary history in the Australasian robins (Passeriformes: Petroicidae) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=61 | issue=3 | pages=726–738 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2011.08.014 | pmid=21867765 | bibcode=2011MolPE..61..726C }}</ref> The yellow-bellied flyrobin is now placed as the only species in the genus ''Cryptomicroeca'' that was introduced in 2012.<ref name=ioc/><ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Christidis | first1=L. | last2=Irestedt | first2=M. | last3=Rowe | first3=D. | last4=Boles | first4=W.E. | last5=Norman | first5=J.A. | year=2012 | title=Circumscription, diagnosis and description of two subfamilies and one genus of Australo-Papuan robins (Aves: Passeriformes: Petroicidae) | journal=Zootaxa | volume=3560 | issue=1 | pages=87–88 | doi=10.11646/zootaxa.3560.1.6 | doi-access=free }}</ref>

==Description==
The yellow-bellied robin is a medium-sized Australasian robin, {{cvt|14|–|15|cm}} in length and weighing around {{cvt|12|g}}. The [[plumage]] is similar to members of the genus ''Eopsaltria'': dark olive-grey back, tail and wings, grey head and chest with a slightly lighter throat, and yellow belly and rump. The legs are grey.
